# **Computational Stylometrics and Behavioral Choice Analysis: A Multidimensional Framework for Evaluating Large Language Model Text Generation and Decision-Making Baselines**

The rapid escalation of Large Language Models (LLMs) in generating human-like text has made traditional natural language processing evaluation metrics increasingly obsolete.1 Historically, the evaluation of text similarity and translation quality relied on lexical overlap metrics, primarily the Longest Common Subsequence (LCS) algorithms inherent to ROUGE and BLEU.2 While these subsequence measures are computationally straightforward, they are fundamentally limited because they evaluate text through exact sequence alignment, ignoring deep semantic relations, grammatical structures, and stylistic variations.2  
As modern generative systems like ChatGPT produce fluent, highly standardized outputs, a clear need has emerged for multidimensional stylometric, psycholinguistic, and behavioral frameworks.1 These frameworks assess text along independent axes—lexical diversity, syntactic complexity, semantic variation, and revealed behavioral choices.1 By moving beyond simple subsequence lengths, researchers can extract the underlying linguistic fingerprints of specific authors or models, compare generative outputs against human writing, and evaluate whether an AI agent's latent decision-making behaviors align with human social, emotional, and economic baselines.1

## **Beyond Subsequence Matching: The Limitations of ROUGE and the Transition to Multi-Feature Analysis**

The primary limitation of ROUGE-L, which utilizes the Longest Common Subsequence between a candidate text and a gold reference, is its insensitivity to grammatical paraphrasing, lexical substitution, and structural reordering.2 A text generated by ChatGPT can convey identical semantic meaning to a human reference while using entirely different vocabulary and syntactic configurations.2 In such scenarios, ROUGE scores drop precipitously, misrepresenting a high-quality, fluent generation as a failure.2 Conversely, a highly repetitive or incoherent sentence that happens to share a long subsequence with the reference can achieve an artificially inflated ROUGE score.2  
To overcome these limitations, modern text analysis utilizes multi-feature profiling.1 Stylometry operates on the principle that every author, whether human or machine, possesses a unique writing style that can be quantified through statistical patterns.9 This quantitative analysis has evolved from simple character-level assessments to sophisticated models capturing complex, hierarchical syntax and semantic distributions.9

| Feature Class | Key Analytical Limitations of ROUGE-L | Multi-Feature Alternative | Target Metric / Framework |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Lexical** | Punishes synonyms and vocabulary variation.2 | Global and local lexical richness metrics.13 | MATTR, MTLD, HD-D.15 |
| **Syntactic** | Insensitive to grammatical structures and dependencies.17 | Morphological distributions and POS tag transitions.17 | POS n-grams, CR-POS.7 |
| **Semantic** | Fails to recognize conceptual equivalence without token overlap.7 | Neural sentence and token embeddings.7 | Embedding Diversity, DCScore.7 |
| **Psycholinguistic** | Blind to emotional valence, social clout, and cognitive styles.19 | Dictionary-based psycholinguistic mapping.19 | LIWC-22 Summary Variables.19 |
| **Coherence** | Evaluates local n-grams without tracking logical narrative flow.23 | Discourse entity tracking and relation mapping.25 | Entity Grid, Discourse Role Matrix.24 |

## **Systematizing Word-Level Stylometric and Structural Features**

Comprehensive stylometric analysis evaluates text across several linguistic layers, using features categorized into lexical, syntactic, structural, content-specific, and idiosyncratic groups.10 This approach is exemplified by the "Writeprints" feature set, a large-scale stylistic framework designed for robust authorship attribution and profiling.10

### **Static and Dynamic Features**

The Writeprints model differentiates between static features, which remain relatively stable across domains, and dynamic features, which adapt based on context.10 Static markers include letter frequencies, vocabulary richness indices, and punctuation distributions.10 Dynamic markers track the variation in an author's stylistic patterns over time or across different platforms.10

### **Function Words versus Content Words**

A fundamental distinction in word-level feature analysis is the separation of function words from content words.9 Function words—including articles, prepositions, pronouns, auxiliary verbs, and conjunctions—are highly frequent, topic-independent grammatical particles.9 Because they are used unconsciously, function word distributions serve as highly stable, domain-general indicators of writing style.9  
Conversely, content words—such as nouns, adjectives, and verbs—are highly dependent on the topic of discussion.12 Relying heavily on content words for stylometric analysis introduces thematic bias, which degrades authorship verification performance when analyzing texts across different genres or domains.5

### **Open versus Closed Word Classes**

Linguistic frameworks also categorize vocabulary into open and closed word classes.28 Open classes (nouns, adjectives, adverbs, and verbs) readily accept new words and vary cross-linguistically based on semantic requirements.28 Closed classes (pronouns, determiners, prepositions, and postpositions) are highly language-specific and structurally rigid.28  
Analyzing the relative distributions of closed classes provides deep insights into syntactic transformations.28 For instance, objective texts tend to exhibit a high density of nouns and prepositions, whereas subjective, human narratives often feature an elevated frequency of personal pronouns, indicating a more self-referential writing style.28

## **Lexical Diversity and Length-Normalization Mathematical Models**

Lexical diversity measures the richness and distributional properties of an author's vocabulary.13 When comparing ChatGPT-generated text against a human baseline, simple vocabulary counts are easily skewed.14 Advanced metrics utilize mathematical formulations designed to isolate vocabulary variety and evenness while mitigating the confounding variable of text length.13

### **Mathematical Formulations of Lexical Richness**

The fundamental Type-Token Ratio (TTR) is formulated as:  
![][image1]  
where ![][image2] represents the number of unique word types and ![][image3] represents the total number of tokens.14 Because writers must repeat common function words as a text grows, TTR decreases monotonically as ![][image3] increases, rendering it highly sensitive to document length.14  
To resolve this length dependency, the Moving Average Type-Token Ratio (MATTR) calculates the average TTR across a sliding window of a fixed size ![][image4].15 For a text of ![][image3] tokens, the formulation is:  
![][image5]  
where ![][image6] is the number of unique types within the ![][image7]\-th sliding window of size ![][image4].16 While MATTR successfully normalizes for length, it introduces a sequential bias.16 Tokens situated near the middle of a text segment are featured in up to ![][image4] windows, whereas tokens at the absolute extremes (positions ![][image8] and ![][image3]) are featured in fewer windows.16 Specifically, the number of windows a token appears in is determined by taking the absolute difference plus one between its position and the closest extreme position (![][image8] or ![][image3]).16 This results in middle tokens exerting a significantly larger impact on the overall MATTR score than those at the extremes.16  
The Measure of Textual Lexical Diversity (MTLD) approaches length invariance by calculating the average number of consecutive words required for the TTR to drop below a predefined stabilization threshold, typically set at ![][image9].15 When the running TTR falls below this threshold, a factor count is incremented, the TTR evaluations are reset, and the process repeats.15 The final score is calculated as:  
![][image10]  
To ensure structural robustness, the analysis is executed on both the original text and its reversed sequence, with the final MTLD score being the mean of the forward and backward runs.15  
The Hypergeometric Distribution D (HD-D) provides an exact probabilistic alternative by evaluating the full type-token distribution of the text without relying on sequential sliding windows.16 HD-D calculates the expected number of unique words that would appear in a random sample of size ![][image4] (conventionally ![][image11]) drawn without replacement from the total token pool ![][image3].30 The probability ![][image12] of drawing at least one token of a specific type ![][image13] is calculated, and the contribution of each type is summed to yield a unified index 32:  
![][image14]  
where ![][image15] represents the set of all unique word types in the text.32 This global approach assigns equal weight to every token, regardless of its sequential position, making it a highly stable indicator of vocabulary richness.16

| Lexical Metric | Mathematical Formulation / Concept | Length-Invariance Profile | Key Analytical Strengths | Key Analytical Limitations |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Type-Token Ratio (TTR)** | **![][image16]** 30 | Low; highly sensitive to ![][image3].14 | Simple calculation.14 | Monotonically decreases with text size.14 |
| **Moving Average TTR (MATTR)** | **![][image17]** 16 | High; completely normalizes to window size ![][image4].16 | Tracks local lexical fluctuation.16 | Overweights middle tokens; ignores repetitions past window length.16 |
| **Measure of Textual Lexical Diversity (MTLD)** | **![][image18]** (Forward & Reversed mean).15 | High; highly resilient to variations in ![][image3].15 | Captures text-wide lexical decay factors.15 | Computationally complex; sensitive to local repetitive anomalies.15 |
| **Hypergeometric Distribution D (HD-D)** | **![][image19]** (for draw size ![][image4]).32 | Complete; theoretically robust length normalization.16 | Treats all tokens equally; global measure.16 | Discards all syntax, narrative flow, and sequential context.16 |
| **Maas Index** | **![][image20]** 15 | Moderate; assumes logarithmic curve fitting.15 | Linearizes the relationship between ![][image3] and TTR.15 | Fails when TTR curves deviate from strict logarithmic growth.15 |
| **Yule's K** | **![][image21]** 30 | High; designed around vocabulary stability.30 | Less sensitive to length than TTR.30 | Insensitive to rare types; heavily weighted by frequent terms.30 |

## **Syntactic Complexity and Grammatical Standardization Baselines**

Syntactic complexity reflects an author’s or model's reliance on specific grammatical structures, sentence patterns, and morphological distributions.18 Analyzing syntax allows for the identification of structural markers that distinguish natural human writing from the highly standardized outputs typical of generative models.1

### **Grammatical Standardization in ChatGPT**

A primary distinguishing feature of LLM-generated text is grammatical hyper-standardization.1 Due to training objectives that optimize for next-token probability prediction, models tend to produce highly conventionalized grammatical structures.1 This structural homogeneity can be identified using the Part-of-Speech Compression Ratio (CR-POS).7  
To compute CR-POS, raw text is processed through a POS tagger, replacing each word with its corresponding morphological tag (e.g., converting "The quick brown fox jumps" to DET ADJ ADJ NOUN VERB).7 The resulting string of POS tags is compressed using a lossless compression algorithm, such as Gzip.7 Syntactic diversity is then calculated as the reciprocal of the compression ratio:  
![][image22]  
Because LLMs consistently rely on a constrained set of repetitive syntactic templates, their POS-tagged outputs exhibit high redundancy, yielding a high compression ratio and a significantly lower syntactic diversity score compared to human authors.1

### **Shallow and Deep Syntactic Features**

Syntactic complexity can be extracted using both shallow and deep representations:

* **POS Tag n-grams:** Captures the transitional probabilities of syntactic classes (e.g., the likelihood of a determiner being followed directly by an adjective versus a noun).18 This is highly robust for large-scale stylometric attribution.18  
* **Sentence Length Distribution:** Humans exhibit high variance in sentence length, using combinations of short, punchy statements and long, clause-heavy sentences to create rhythm.1 LLMs default to highly consistent, standardized sentence lengths.1  
* **Clause Density / Complexity:** Evaluates structural sophistication by tracking metrics like the ratio of dependent clauses per clause or the mean length of T-units.17  
* **ASCII Dimensionality Reduction:** Utilizes Principal Component Analysis (PCA) over raw character ASCII representations to project sentence structure into a low-dimensional space, providing a resource-efficient syntactic representation to identify corpus imbalances.34

## **Psycholinguistic Profiling and Semantic Diversity Models**

While lexical and syntactic metrics analyze the structural surface of a text, semantic and psycholinguistic features capture the underlying meaning, cognitive processes, and emotional valence embedded within writing styles.7

### **Psycholinguistic Profiling via LIWC**

Linguistic Inquiry and Word Count (LIWC) is a validated psycholinguistic dictionary-based framework that maps text tokens to over 100 cognitive, emotional, and social dimensions.19 It operates by matching words against curated dictionaries and calculating category-wise frequencies as a percentage of the total word count.19  
LIWC-22 introduces four core summary variables, calculated as proprietary standardized percentiles (ranging from 1 to 99\) derived from underlying empirical algorithms based on positive emotion, negative emotion, cognitive processes, affiliation, and status-seeking language 20:

* **Analytical Thinking:** Measures the degree of formal, logical, and hierarchical thinking versus narrative, intuitive processing.22 High scores correlate with the use of articles, prepositions, and complex structural words.22  
* **Clout:** Quantifies the perspective of leadership, status, and social influence projected by the writing.22 It is marked by a high frequency of second-person pronouns ("you") and first-person plural pronouns ("we") alongside a low frequency of first-person singular pronouns ("I", "me").20  
* **Authenticity:** Evaluates the degree of personal honesty, openness, and psychological vulnerability.22 Authentic text exhibits a higher density of self-referential pronouns and concrete, context-specific language.22  
* **Emotional Tone:** Synthesizes the balance between positive and negative affect.22 High scores (\>50) denote a positive, optimistic orientation, while lower scores (\<50) denote negativity, anxiety, or hostility.20

These summary measures provide clear stylistic boundaries.19 LLMs consistently score exceptionally high in *Analytical Thinking* due to their structured, formal training corpora, but often exhibit unnaturally suppressed variance in *Authenticity* and *Emotional Tone* compared to human-written benchmarks.1

### **Semantic Diversity Metrics**

To assess vocabulary variety without being constrained by exact lexical matches, researchers utilize semantic embeddings.7 This allows the framework to detect redundancy even when different words are used to express identical concepts.7

* **Embedding Cosine Distance:** Pairwise cosine similarity is calculated across the sentence embeddings of a generated corpus.7 To convert this into a standardized metric where higher scores denote greater variety, the mean pairwise similarity is subtracted from unity 7:

![][image23]  
where ![][image24] and ![][image25] represent embedding vectors and ![][image26] is the number of text segments.7

* **DCScore:** This metric normalizes the pairwise similarity matrix of embeddings using a softmax function.7 Ideally, a diverse text should display high self-similarity and zero similarity to other texts.7 The softmax function converts these similarities into relative probabilities, and the final dataset diversity score is computed as the mean of the diagonal of the normalized matrix.7  
* **Cluster Inertia:** Text embeddings are partitioned into ![][image27] distinct clusters (typically ![][image28]) using algorithms like K-Means.7 The sum of the squared distances between each embedding and its respective cluster centroid is calculated.7 Highly diverse datasets, containing a broad spectrum of semantic topics, result in scattered embeddings and a significantly higher inertia score.7

## **Local and Global Coherence Frameworks**

Coherence refers to the logical and meaningful connections that bind different parts of a text into a unified whole.23 It operates at two primary levels: local coherence, which focuses on the sequential relationships between adjacent sentences, and global coherence, which governs the overarching macro-structure and thematic unity of the entire document.23

### **Modeling Coherence in NLP**

Computational linguistics models coherence using several established structural frameworks:

1. **The Entity Grid Model:** This model abstracts a text into a two-dimensional grid where rows represent sentences and columns represent discourse entities.23 Each cell records the syntactic role of an entity in a given sentence (e.g., Subject $$, Object ![][image29], Oblique ![][image30], or Absent ![][image31]\]).26 Transition patterns of these entities across adjacent sentences (e.g., ![][image32], ![][image33]) are extracted as feature vectors to train classifiers that distinguish coherent texts from shuffled permutations in a standard **Shuffle Test**.23  
2. **Discourse Role Matrix:** An advancement of the entity grid that integrates parsed discourse relations (e.g., Cause, Contrast, Elaboration) directly alongside entity transitions.24 By capturing both the topical focus (entities) and the rhetorical connections (discourse relations), this matrix models the complex strategies writers employ simultaneously to ensure coherence.25

## **Evaluating and Comparing Writing Style against ChatGPT Baselines**

Evaluating an unknown text's writing style against a baseline output of ChatGPT requires a combination of information-theoretic, stylistic transfer, and competitive retrieval methods.9 These frameworks move beyond simple visual comparisons to quantify the precise mathematical distance between a text and the model's latent stylistic distribution.9

### **Cross-Entropy and Perplexity-Based Stylometry**

The core concept of language-model-based stylometry is that a neural model trained on or embodying a specific writing style will predict text written in that style with higher accuracy than text written in other styles.9 When an LLM like GPT-2 is trained or fine-tuned on the works of a specific author, its cross-entropy loss serves as a direct measure of stylometric distance.9  
To compare an unknown text against a ChatGPT baseline, researchers evaluate the cross-entropy loss and perplexity (PPL) assigned by the model when predicting the text.2 To ensure that each token contributes equally to the computed loss and to avoid length-related biases, the evaluation set is partitioned into fixed-size chunks, typically 1024-token segments.9 A lower cross-entropy loss indicates that the text aligns closely with the model's internal writing patterns, serving as a powerful indicator of stylistic similarity.9

  \+-------------------------------------------------------------+  
  |                        INPUT TEXT                           |  
  \+-------------------------------------------------------------+  
                                 |  
                                 v  
  \+-------------------------------------------------------------+  
  |                     SEGMENT PARTITIONING                    |  
  |             \- Partitioned into 1024-token chunks            |  
  |             \- Ensures equal contribution to loss            |  
  \+-------------------------------------------------------------+  
                                 |  
                                 v  
  \+-------------------------------------------------------------+  
  |                     PREDICTION ENGINE                       |  
  |           \- Evaluated by ChatGPT baseline model             |  
  \+-------------------------------------------------------------+  
         |                                             |  
         v                                             v  
  \+-----------------------------+               \+-----------------------------+  
  |    High Cross-Entropy /     |               |     Low Cross-Entropy /     |  
  |     Elevated Perplexity     |               |      Reduced Perplexity     |  
  \+-----------------------------+               \+-----------------------------+  
         |                                             |  
         v                                             v  
  \+-----------------------------+               \+-----------------------------+  
  |     Stylistic Divergence    |               |     Stylistic Similarity    |  
  |  (Human-authored or unique) |               |     (ChatGPT-generated)     |  
  \+-----------------------------+               \+-----------------------------+

### **One-Shot Style Transfer (OSST) and Neutral Style Conversion**

To isolate an author's writing style from the thematic content of their text, researchers utilize One-Shot Style Transfer (OSST) and neutral style conversion.38 When attempting to attribute authorship under cross-domain distribution shifts (e.g., comparing informal emails to formal prose), direct feature comparison is often confounded by topic-specific vocabulary.27  
To resolve this, the text is first converted into a "neutral style" by prompting an LLM to strip away stylistic markers, emotional tone, and idiosyncratic patterns while preserving the core factual content.38 Once a neutral version of the text is established, the target model's style is injected through an x-shot style transfer prompt.38 Comparing the original text against these style-transferred versions allows researchers to analyze the precise modifications made to vocabulary, syntax, and phrasing, establishing a highly accurate stylistic baseline.38

### **Double-Blind Tournament-Style Attribution**

When scaling authorship analysis to large corpora containing thousands of potential candidate texts, direct LLM comparison becomes computationally expensive.37 To address this, the Double-Blind Anonymity System (DAS) implements a multi-stage progressive elimination strategy 37:

* **Coarse Filtering:** An embedding-based retrieval module first narrows down the candidate search space from a large corpus of up to ![][image34] texts to a more tractable subset of ![][image35] likely candidates.37  
* **Tournament-Style Attribution (TSA):** The remaining candidate texts are randomly partitioned into small, fixed-size groups.37 An LLM is prompted to perform a one-to-many comparison, selecting the text within each group that is most likely written by the same author as a query text.37 The surviving candidates are iteratively re-grouped and re-compared through successive tournament rounds until a ranked top-k list of stylistic matches is generated.37

## **Model Decision-Making Choices versus Human Baselines**

Evaluating language models extends beyond stylistic text features to the analysis of their latent decision-making behaviors and choices.6 Grounded in behavioral economics and psychology, this analysis evaluates how closely a model's revealed actions mirror human social norms, risk aversion, and emotional dynamics.6

### **Transformational Pipeline for Behavioral Assessment**

To accurately compare the choices made by a model against human baselines, researchers must move away from self-report questionnaires.6 Because LLMs often exhibit construct validity issues (e.g., claiming to be highly empathetic on a Likert scale while generating detached, uncaring responses), researchers implement a multi-stage pipeline to transform self-report items into Situational Judgment Tests (SJTs) that observe revealed behavior.6  
First, validated preference statements are extracted from psychological frameworks (e.g., Trait Emotional Intelligence).6 These statements are subjected to a behavioral filter to remove biological reflexes (e.g., "I sweat when anxious") and are then reframed into advisory declarations.6 For example, the statement *"When I am upset at someone, I try to put myself in their shoes"* is reframed as *"I recommend that when a person is upset at someone, they should try to put themselves in their shoes."*.6  
Using this reframed advisory statement, multiple concrete, realistic user-assistant scenarios are generated, presenting a clear dilemma with two opposing reference action legs:

1. **Support Action Leg:** Aligns directly with the target positive trait.6  
2. **Oppose Action Leg:** Opposes the target trait.6

Rather than restricting the model to a multiple-choice format, which can introduce formatting and alphabetical biases, the model is prompted with the SJT and permitted to write a free-form, open-ended response.6 To bypass natural hedging, prompts explicitly instruct the model to commit to a single course of action.6 An LLM-as-a-Judge maps this free-form response back to either the Support or Oppose action.6  
The Trait-Positive Rate (TPR) is calculated for both humans (based on the consensus of 10 independent annotators) and the model (by sampling the model 20 times per scenario at a temperature of ![][image36]).6 Trait Misalignment is then quantified as:  
![][image37]

### **Discrepancy under Low Human Consensus**

A key behavioral trend identified through this framework is that in scenarios where human consensus is low, LLMs consistently exhibit overconfidence, clustering heavily around a single response instead of reflecting natural human preference variance.6

  \+-------------------------------------------------------------+  
  |              PSYCHOMETRIC PREFERENCE STATEMENT              |  
  |     "When I am upset, I try to understand other perspectives" |  
  \+-------------------------------------------------------------+  
                                 |  
                                 v  
  \+-------------------------------------------------------------+  
  |                     ADVISORY REFRAMING                      |  
  | "I recommend that when a person is upset, they should try..." |  
  \+-------------------------------------------------------------+  
                                 |  
                                 v  
  \+-------------------------------------------------------------+  
  |             SITUATIONAL JUDGMENT TEST (SJT)                 |  
  |      \- Generates realistic user-assistant dilemmas          |  
  |      \- Establishes Support vs. Oppose action legs           |  
  \+-------------------------------------------------------------+  
         |                                             |  
         v (Annotator Distribution)                    v (Model Sampling, T=1.0)  
  \+-----------------------------+               \+-----------------------------+  
  |   Human Preference Baseline |               |     Revealed Model Choice   |  
  |     \- 10 annotators per SJT |               |     \- 20 runs per scenario  |  
  \+-----------------------------+               \+-----------------------------+  
         \\                                             /  
          \\                                           /  
           v                                         v  
  \+---------------------------------------------------------------------------+  
  |                         TRAIT MISALIGNMENT SCORE                          |  
  |             | Human Trait Positive Rate \- Model Trait Positive Rate |      |  
  \+---------------------------------------------------------------------------+

### **Cumulative Prospect Theory and Holt-Laury Parameters**

Using multiple-choice list experiments, researchers evaluate LLM choices under risk and uncertainty through the lens of Cumulative Prospect Theory (CPT).8 Models are tested using classic paradigms such as the Holt and Laury lottery menu, which presents a list of ordered choices between a safe lottery and an increasingly risky lottery.40 This allows researchers to estimate three critical dimensions:

* **Risk Preference (![][image38]):** Modeled using a Constant Relative Risk Aversion (CRRA) utility function:

![][image39]  
where ![][image40] indicates risk aversion, ![][image41] denotes risk-seeking behavior, and the crossover point in the lottery menu determines the parameter value.40

* **Loss Aversion (![][image42]):** Evaluated using a mixed-lottery acceptance task with a ![][image43] chance of a fixed gain and a varying loss leg.40 Loss aversion parameters are recovered from the model's indifference point, with ![][image44] indicating that losses loom larger than equivalent gains.8  
* **Probability Weighting:** Quantifies the tendency of decision-makers to systematically distort objective probabilities, typically overweighting small probabilities and underweighting moderate-to-high ones.8

Advanced models generally align with human baselines by displaying risk and loss aversion.8 However, their decision-making parameters vary dynamically depending on socio-demographic variables embedded directly into the prompts, occasionally revealing biases such as heightened risk aversion when simulated attributes represent marginalized groups.8

| Decision-Making Parameter | Underlying Mathematical / Theoretical Model | Target Behavioral Variable | Human Baseline Values | Model-to-Model Variations |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Risk Preference (![][image38])** | CRRA Utility: ![][image45].40 | Crossover point in paired lottery menu.40 | Median ![][image46] (mostly risk-averse).40 | Gemini-1.5 Pro displays textbook rationality; Claude-3-Opus exhibits high risk aversion.8 |
| **Loss Aversion (![][image42])** | Mixed-lottery utility formulation.40 | Indifference point on gain/loss legs.40 | Median ![][image47].40 | ChatGPT-4.0-Turbo mirrors human loss aversion; smaller models diverge significantly.8 |
| **Probability Weighting** | Cumulative Prospect Theory (CPT).8 | Probability distortion curves.42 | Overweighting small probabilities; underweighting large ones.8 | LLMs consistently distort low-probability events, displaying higher variance in highly uncertain scenarios.8 |
| **Strategic Choice (SBG)** | JSD over Influence Regulation Process (IRP).39 | Conflict resolution strategy distributions.39 | Dynamic combination of collaborative and defensive strategies.39 | Claude-3.7-Sonnet matches human strategic play; GPT-4.1 excels in emotional pacing.39 |

## **Synthesis and Comprehensive Textual Feature Registry**

To provide a unified toolkit for computational text analysis, the following table synthesizes the complete array of lexical, syntactic, semantic, and behavioral features. It outlines their primary extraction mechanisms, quantitative indicators, and specific analytical functions in distinguishing human style from generative baselines.

| Category | Feature Name | Primary Extraction Mechanism | Key Quantitative / Mathematical Indicator | Target Analytical Function |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Lexical** | Moving Average TTR (MATTR).15 | Overlapping sliding-window token analysis.15 | ![][image17].16 | Tracks localized vocabulary richness while controlling for text length.16 |
| **Lexical** | Measure of Textual Lexical Diversity (MTLD).15 | Sequential TTR decay factor calculation.15 | Mean of forward and backward running ![][image48].15 | Evaluates vocabulary variety over long passages with high length invariance.15 |
| **Lexical** | Hypergeometric Distribution D (HD-D).16 | Probabilistic drawing of types without replacement.16 | Sum of ![][image49] across all unique word types.32 | Provides a global, position-invariant baseline of vocabulary breadth.16 |
| **Lexical** | Distinct n-grams.7 | Rolling n-gram frequency extraction.7 | Ratio of unique n-grams to total occurrences.7 | Detects vocabulary repetition and local phrase redundancy.7 |
| **Lexical** | N-gram Entropy.7 | Probability distribution modeling of n-grams.7 | Shannon entropy normalized by uniform distribution upper bound.7 | Measures vocabulary balance and distributional uniformity.7 |
| **Lexical** | Compression Ratio.7 | Lossless compression (Gzip) of raw text string.7 | ![][image50].7 | Quantifies raw surface-level redundancy and pattern repetition.7 |
| **Syntactic** | POS n-grams.17 | Morphological tagging followed by transition sequence count.17 | Relative frequency distributions of sequential tag classes.17 | Maps structural style, syntactic preferences, and grammatical transitions.17 |
| **Syntactic** | POS Compression Ratio (CR-POS).7 | Lossless compression applied to POS tag sequences.7 | ![][image51].7 | Reveals underlying structural template replication and grammatical monotony.7 |
| **Syntactic** | Syntactic Complexity Ratios.17 | Deep dependency or constituency parsing.18 | Ratio of dependent clauses; maximum parse tree depth.17 | Quantifies grammatical sophistication and sentence structure variety.18 |
| **Semantic** | Embedding Diversity.7 | Cosine similarity matrix of sentence embeddings.7 | ![][image52].7 | Measures variety in thematic content and overall text meaning.7 |
| **Semantic** | DCScore.7 | Softmax normalization of pairwise embedding similarities.7 | Mean of the diagonal of the normalized similarity matrix.7 | Normalizes similarity profiles to measure true conceptual uniqueness.7 |
| **Semantic** | Cluster Inertia.7 | K-Means clustering over document embeddings.7 | Sum of squared distances to cluster centroids.7 | Measures the presence of multiple topics in a text corpus.7 |
| **Psycholinguistic** | LIWC Summary Metrics.19 | Dictionary-based keyword density analysis.19 | Standardized percentiles (1–99) for Analytic, Clout, Authenticity, Tone.22 | Profiles cognitive styles, emotional valence, and projected status.19 |
| **Psycholinguistic** | Functional Pronoun Ratios.19 | Frequency analysis of specific function word groups.20 | Ratio of first-person singular ("I") to plural ("We") and second-person ("You").20 | Tracks social distance, confidence, and self-focus.20 |
| **Coherence** | Entity Grid Transitions.23 | Syntactic parsing of discourse entity sequences.26 | Distribution vectors of transitions (e.g., Subject ![][image53] Object).26 | Models local structural transitions and sentence connectivity.23 |
| **Coherence** | Discourse Role Matrix.24 | Joint parsing of entity transitions and rhetorical relations.25 | Multi-dimensional matrix tracking discourse roles and relations.25 | Integrates semantic topic tracking with explicit rhetorical connections.25 |
| **Behavioral** | Risk Preference (![][image38]).8 | Multiple-choice list lottery menus (e.g., Holt-Laury).40 | Constant Relative Risk Aversion (CRRA) utility coefficient ![][image38].40 | Pinpoints model risk behavior under objective uncertainty.8 |
| **Behavioral** | Loss Aversion (![][image42]).8 | Mixed-lottery acceptance tasks.40 | Indifference point coefficient ![][image42].40 | Evaluates relative sensitivity to losses versus gains.8 |
| **Behavioral** | Trait Misalignment.6 | Situational Judgment Tests with advisory-reframed prompts.6 | $ | \\text{Human TPR} \- \\text{Model TPR} |
| **Behavioral** | Strategic Behavior Gap (SBG).39 | Utterance strategy annotation in conflict dialogues.39 | JSD over 9-way Influence Regulation Process distributions.39 | Quantifies alignment of tactical negotiation styles.39 |

## **Conclusions**

Evaluating writing style and behavioral choices requires moving beyond superficial string-overlap metrics such as ROUGE.2 Incorporating a comprehensive framework that spans lexical, syntactic, semantic, psycholinguistic, and behavioral layers provides a highly nuanced toolkit for text analysis.1  
At the lexical and syntactic levels, length-invariant metrics like MTLD and HD-D, combined with structural measures like the Part-of-Speech Compression Ratio (CR-POS), reveal distinct stylistic markers.7 These markers are highly effective at separating the standardized, predictable patterns of machine-generated text from the highly variable style of human authors.1 At the same time, semantic and psycholinguistic profiling through LIWC and embedding diversity measures can identify differences in cognitive structure, thematic variety, and emotional framing.7  
Finally, evaluating language models using Situational Judgment Tests (SJTs) and behavioral economics parameters (such as the Holt-Laury risk coefficient ![][image38] or loss aversion ![][image42]) highlights important differences in decision-making.6 These methods demonstrate that while advanced models can mimic human-like risk aversion and social choices, they frequently show overconfidence in low-consensus situations and exhibit systematic variations based on socio-demographic prompts.6 Using this multi-layered framework, researchers can construct precise stylistic and behavioral profiles of language models, supporting robust forensic authorship attribution, synthetic text detection, and behavioral safety alignment.1

#### **Works cited**

1. Stylometry recognizes human and LLM-generated texts in short samples \- arXiv, accessed June 4, 2026, [https://arxiv.org/html/2507.00838v2](https://arxiv.org/html/2507.00838v2)
